display_name: Output drops with low average utilization mean microbursts — the average is hiding the burst
context: An interface shows output/queue drops yet the reported utilization (averaged over the polling interval) is well below line rate
observation: Egress drops are incrementing while 5-minute or even 30-second average utilization looks comfortable (e.g. 30-40%)
naive_interpretation: The drops must be a counter bug or a hardware fault, because the link clearly is not congested
correct_interpretation: Averaged utilization smooths out short, intense bursts. A microburst can fill the egress buffer at line rate for a few milliseconds, causing tail drops, while the average over a 30-300 second window stays low. The interface is momentarily congested even though it looks idle on average. The drops are real and the cause is instantaneous buffer exhaustion, not a faulty counter. Buffer/queue depth telemetry, not average bandwidth, is what reveals it.
why: Interface utilization counters are averages over the polling interval; sub-second bursts are invisible at 30s-5min granularity
Egress buffers are small and fill at line rate during a burst, so a few milliseconds of over-rate causes tail drops
The mismatch between non-zero drops and low average utilization is the classic signature of microbursting, not a counter error
Detecting it requires high-resolution queue-depth or buffer-latency telemetry rather than averaged bandwidth

confirmation_commands.purpose: Show per-queue buffer usage and tail-drop counts that reveal momentary congestion the average hides
Surface microburst events captured at high resolution on platforms that support burst detection
Compare output drops against the averaged load to confirm drops persist while the average stays low

traps: Dismissing real egress drops as a counter bug because the averaged utilization looks low
Trusting 5-minute averages to rule out congestion when bursts last milliseconds
Adding bandwidth without buffer/QoS tuning when the issue is burst shape, not sustained rate
Not collecting high-resolution queue telemetry, the only view that shows the burst
